excerpts.text: I was determined that the rising generation of China should enjoy the same educational advantages that I had enjoyed.
excerpts.source_title: My Life in China and America

excerpts.text: Through Western education, China can become a strong, modern nation without losing her ancient virtues.
excerpts.source_title: My Life in China and America

excerpts.text: I entered Yale College in 1850 and graduated in 1854, the first Chinaman who had ever been graduated from Yale.
excerpts.source_title: My Life in China and America

excerpts.text: My object in getting an education was not to gain personal advantage but to do China the most good.
excerpts.source_title: My Life in China and America

excerpts.text: At the Morrison School in Macau I first encountered the Christian faith. It was through the kindness of missionaries that I received the foundation of my education.
excerpts.source_title: My Life in China and America

excerpts.text: I was baptized at the age of seventeen. The Christian principles I learned shaped everything that followed in my life.
excerpts.source_title: My Life in China and America

excerpts.text: The Chinese Educational Mission was the great work of my life. I selected 120 boys to study in New England, living with American families.
excerpts.source_title: My Life in China and America

excerpts.text: Many of the boys became interested in Christianity through their host families. This alarmed the imperial supervisors in Hartford.
excerpts.source_title: My Life in China and America

excerpts.text: When the Chinese government recalled the students in 1881, it broke my heart. The mission was destroyed by fear — fear that Western ideas would corrupt Chinese youth.
excerpts.source_title: My Life in China and America

excerpts.text: I have lived between two civilizations. I know the best of both. My life's work has been to bring them together.
excerpts.source_title: My Life in China and America

excerpts.text: At Yale I learned not only science and letters but the dignity of the individual — a concept foreign to imperial China but entirely consistent with the Gospel.
excerpts.source_title: My Life in China and America

excerpts.text: The ship captain asked if I expected to make an impression on the great Chinese Empire. I told him I expected God would.
excerpts.source_title: Attributed, referencing Morrison's famous exchange

excerpts.text: Education is the key to China's future. But education without moral foundation produces clever men without conscience.
excerpts.source_title: My Life in China and America

excerpts.text: I naturalized as an American citizen. But I never ceased to be Chinese. The two identities enriched each other.
excerpts.source_title: My Life in China and America

excerpts.text: The boys who returned to China from the Educational Mission became engineers, diplomats, and naval officers. They modernized China despite never finishing their studies.
excerpts.source_title: My Life in China and America

excerpts.text: Sun Yat-sen carried forward what I had begun — the vision of a modern, reformed China. He too was shaped by Christian education abroad.
excerpts.source_title: Late reflections (attributed)

excerpts.text: I believe that Chinese students sent abroad will always return changed. The question is whether the change will be deep enough to last.
excerpts.source_title: My Life in China and America

excerpts.text: New England hospitality taught me that Christianity is not a doctrine to be studied but a life to be lived among neighbors.
excerpts.source_title: My Life in China and America

excerpts.text: I spent my final years in Hartford, Connecticut — where the Educational Mission began and ended. My life came full circle.
excerpts.source_title: My Life in China and America

excerpts.text: If I could do it again, I would still send those 120 boys. Even an incomplete education planted seeds that grew beyond anything I imagined.
excerpts.source_title: Late correspondence
